pattern: Greedy column assignment: force both rows to 1 where the column sum is 2 and both to 0 where it is 0; then distribute the remaining budget from the colsum=1 columns — fill the upper row first until its quota is met, then the lower row.
hints: Columns where the sum is 2 or 0 are uniquely determined — no choice exists; handle those first and deduct from the row budgets accordingly.

After the forced columns are placed, the remaining columns all have sum 1 and represent slots where exactly one row gets a 1; assign them greedily to the upper row until its remaining quota reaches zero, then to the lower row.

Before building the result, verify feasibility: the total of all column sums must equal upper + lower, and after placing the forced columns the leftover budgets must be non-negative and sum to exactly the number of sum-1 columns.

edge_inputs.description: all columns have sum 2 — both rows must be all-ones
all columns have sum 0 — both rows must be all-zeros
row sums add up but forced 2-columns alone exhaust one row budget — no solution
total column sum differs from upper+lower — immediately impossible
single column with sum 1, upper=1, lower=0
single column with sum 1, upper=0, lower=1
mix of 0, 1, and 2 columns all satisfiable

stress.description: large colsum of length 100000 with random mix — exercises the greedy path at scale
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    colsum = [rng.randint(0, 2) for _ in range(n)]
    total = sum(colsum)
    twos = sum(1 for v in colsum if v == 2)
    ones = sum(1 for v in colsum if v == 1)
    # choose upper in a valid range
    upper_min = twos
    upper_max = twos + ones
    if upper_min > upper_max:
        upper = twos
    else:
        upper = rng.randint(upper_min, upper_max)
    lower = total - upper
    return [upper, lower, colsum]
stress.note: always constructed to have a valid solution
